Frame it as observability: "I cannot reason about production without knowing exactly which JVM version and flags are active; `jcmd <pid> VM.version` and `jcmd <pid> VM.flags` are the first tools I check."
jobSwitch.starAnchor: Situation: CI pipeline on a monorepo failed intermittently with UnsupportedClassVersionError after library updates compiled against Java 17 while CI agents ran Java 11. Task: eliminate the version mismatch and prevent recurrence across 40 developer machines and 6 CI agents. Action: added Gradle toolchain declarations to all 12 service build files, pointed Gradle to Foojay resolver for JDK downloads, removed hardcoded JAVA_HOME from CI agent configuration. Result: zero JDK version mismatch incidents in 6 months post-migration; onboarding time for new developers reduced from 2 hours to 15 minutes.

sections.title: MCQ — Introduction to Java: JDK vs JRE vs JVM
sections.description: 30 questions across Basic, Intermediate, and Advanced levels covering JDK components, JVM internals, class loading, JIT, bytecode, and production diagnostics.

questions.question: Which component do you need to **compile** a Java source file into bytecode?

options.A: JRE (Java Runtime Environment)
options.B: JVM (Java Virtual Machine)
options.C: JDK (Java Development Kit)
options.D: JLink

questions.explanation: The JDK bundles `javac` (the compiler). JRE and JVM only provide a runtime; they cannot compile `.java` files. JLink is a JDK tool for creating custom runtime images, not for compiling.

questions.question: What file extension does `javac` produce from a `.java` source file?

options.A: .exe
options.B: .class
options.C: .jar
options.D: .obj

questions.explanation: `javac` compiles Java source into `.class` files containing platform-independent bytecode. `.jar` files are ZIP archives of many `.class` files, not the direct output of a single compilation.

questions.question: Which command checks the version of the Java runtime installed on your machine?

options.A: javac -version
options.B: java --check
options.C: java -version
options.D: jvm -version

questions.explanation: `java -version` prints the runtime version. `javac -version` prints the compiler version — both should match. There is no `jvm` binary; `java` is the launcher that starts the JVM.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `JAVA_HOME`?

options.A: Stores compiled class files
options.B: Points to the JDK installation directory used by build tools
options.C: Specifies the main class to run
options.D: Sets the heap size for the JVM

questions.explanation: `JAVA_HOME` is an environment variable that Maven, Gradle, and many IDEs read to locate the JDK. Setting it incorrectly causes build failures even when `java` appears on PATH from a different installation.

questions.question: Why must a public class named `Calculator` be saved in a file called `Calculator.java`?

options.A: It is a convention but not enforced by the compiler
options.B: The JVM requires it at runtime for class loading
options.C: The compiler enforces that the file name matches the public class name
options.D: Only Maven enforces this, not `javac`

questions.explanation: `javac` enforces that the file name matches the public class name — mismatches produce a compile error: "class Calculator is public, should be declared in a file named Calculator.java". This is a compiler rule, not just a convention.

questions.question: Which statement about the JRE is correct?

options.A: JRE includes `javac` so you can compile Java code
options.B: JRE provides a runtime to execute Java programs but not a compiler
options.C: JRE and JDK are identical; only the name differs
options.D: JRE is only available on Windows

questions.explanation: JRE = JVM + standard libraries. It lets users run compiled Java applications but lacks developer tools like `javac`, `jdb`, or `jcmd`. From JDK 11+ Oracle no longer ships a separate JRE download; the JDK is the baseline.

questions.question:
What does this command do?
```bash
javac Hello.java && java Hello
```

options.A: Compiles Hello.java, then runs the compiled bytecode
options.B: Compiles and runs in a single pass without creating a .class file
options.C: Only compiles — `java Hello` opens a GUI
options.D: Creates a JAR and then executes it

questions.explanation: `javac Hello.java` creates `Hello.class`. `java Hello` launches the JVM and calls the `main` method in `Hello.class`. From Java 11+, `java Hello.java` (source-file mode) can skip the explicit `javac` step, but the two-command form still creates the `.class` file explicitly.

questions.question: What does "platform independence" mean for Java bytecode?

options.A: Java programs never need OS-specific system calls
options.B: The same .class file can run on any OS with a compatible JVM without recompilation
options.C: Java programs run equally fast on every platform
options.D: Bytecode is converted to JavaScript to run in browsers

questions.explanation: Bytecode is an OS-neutral instruction set; any JVM that implements the JVM Specification can execute the same `.class` file on Windows, Linux, or macOS. Caveats: JNI native libraries and OS-specific APIs break portability. Performance varies by JVM and hardware.

questions.question: In the JVM class-loader hierarchy, which loader loads `java.lang.String`?

options.A: Application ClassLoader
options.B: Platform (Extension) ClassLoader
options.C: Bootstrap ClassLoader
options.D: Custom ClassLoader

questions.explanation: The Bootstrap ClassLoader (implemented in native code, not Java) loads core Java API classes from `rt.jar` / `java.base` module — including `java.lang.String`. Parent-delegation ensures your class can never shadow `java.lang.String` because Bootstrap always wins.

questions.question: What is the "parent-delegation model" in class loading?

options.A: Child loaders load classes first, then fall back to parents
options.B: Each loader delegates loading to its parent before trying itself
options.C: Bootstrap loader delegates everything to the Application loader
options.D: Every class is loaded by all loaders simultaneously

questions.explanation: Before a ClassLoader attempts to load a class, it asks its parent. If the parent succeeds, the child is never involved. This guarantees that core JDK classes cannot be replaced by user code, preventing security exploits like shadowing `java.security.SecureRandom`.

questions.question: What does the JVM's bytecode verifier check when loading a class?

options.A: That the source code follows Google Java Style Guide
options.B: That the bytecode is structurally valid: no stack overflows, legal type casts, no uninitialized reads
options.C: That the .class file was compiled with the same `javac` version as the JVM
options.D: That all methods have Javadoc comments

questions.explanation: The verifier enforces JVM Specification safety rules on the bytecode itself — not source-level style. It checks stack depth consistency (StackMapTable), that every field access is type-compatible, and that no uninitialized references are used. `VerifyError` at runtime means a framework (e.g., CGLIB, Lombok) generated invalid bytecode.

questions.question: What error does the JVM throw when a class compiled against Java 17 is run on a Java 11 JVM?

options.A: ClassNotFoundException
options.B: UnsupportedClassVersionError
options.C: VerifyError
options.D: LinkageError

questions.explanation: `UnsupportedClassVersionError` is thrown when the `.class` major version number (61 for Java 17, 55 for Java 11) exceeds what the running JVM supports. Fix: align the compile `--release` flag with the JVM version, or upgrade the JVM. `ClassNotFoundException` means the file is missing, not version-mismatched.

questions.question: Which two JIT compiler tiers does HotSpot use, and what is each optimised for?

options.A: C0 (interpreter) and C1 (native code) — both optimise for throughput
options.B: C1 (fast compilation, quick start) and C2 (heavy optimisation, peak throughput)
options.C: C1 (garbage collection) and C2 (memory allocation)
options.D: C1 (server mode) and C2 (client mode)

questions.explanation: C1 (Client compiler) compiles quickly with basic optimisations — good for startup. C2 (Server compiler) applies aggressive optimisations (loop unrolling, escape analysis, inlining) after profiling hot paths — good for peak throughput. Tiered Compilation (default since Java 8) uses C1 first, then promotes to C2.

questions.question: What does `javap -c Hello` show?

options.A: Java source code of Hello.java
options.B: JVM bytecode instructions for Hello.class
options.C: The runtime heap contents of a running Hello process
options.D: Compilation errors from Hello.java

questions.explanation: `javap -c` disassembles `.class` files into human-readable JVM bytecode mnemonics (e.g., `iconst_1`, `iadd`, `invokevirtual`). Staff engineers use it to verify that compiler optimisations (e.g., string concatenation strategy, switch table vs lookup) occurred as expected.

questions.question: What memory region stores class metadata (method names, field types, constant pool) in Java 8+?

options.A: Young Generation heap
options.B: PermGen (Permanent Generation)
options.C: Metaspace (native memory)
options.D: Code Cache

questions.explanation: Java 8 replaced PermGen with Metaspace, which grows in native (off-heap) memory by default. PermGen had a fixed upper bound causing `OutOfMemoryError: PermGen space` on large deployments. Metaspace is bounded by `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`; without it, a classloader leak will consume all native memory.

questions.question: What is the classpath and when does it matter?

options.A: The directory where `.java` source files are stored
options.B: A list of directories and JARs where the JVM looks for `.class` files at runtime
options.C: The path to the JDK installation on disk
options.D: An environment variable only used by Maven

questions.explanation: Classpath tells both `javac` and `java` where to find compiled classes and library JARs. A missing classpath entry causes `ClassNotFoundException` at runtime. Maven/Gradle manage classpath automatically; manual `-cp` is needed for one-off runs or legacy scripts.

questions.question: A CI pipeline compiles with `javac --release 17` but the Docker image runs `java 11`. What happens at startup?

options.A: The app starts but prints a deprecation warning
options.B: Java 11 automatically down-compiles the bytecode at startup
options.C: The JVM throws `UnsupportedClassVersionError` before `main()` runs
options.D: Only the classes that use Java 17 features fail; others load fine

questions.explanation: The JVM checks the `.class` major version at load time, before executing any user code. Version 61 (Java 17) > 55 (Java 11), so the JVM immediately throws `UnsupportedClassVersionError`. There is no automatic downgrade; you must align the compile target and runtime version.

questions.question: What is the difference between the module path (`--module-path`) and the classpath?

options.A: They are identical; `--module-path` is just an alias introduced in Java 9
options.B: Module path is for JPMS modules that declare `module-info.class`; classpath is for unnamed-module JARs
options.C: Module path is faster because the JVM skips bytecode verification for modules
options.D: Classpath only works on Windows; module path works cross-platform

questions.explanation: Java Platform Module System (Java 9+) introduced `--module-path` for JARs containing `module-info.class`. These modules declare explicit `requires`/`exports`, giving strong encapsulation. Regular JARs without `module-info` go on the classpath as the "unnamed module". Most Spring Boot / Jakarta EE apps still use the classpath.

questions.question: Which Gradle build file snippet ensures the project always compiles and runs with JDK 17, regardless of `JAVA_HOME`?

options.A:
```groovy
sourceCompatibility = "17"
```
options.C:
```groovy
compileJava.options.release = 17
```
options.D:
```groovy
system.setProperty("java.version", "17")
```

questions.explanation: Gradle toolchain declaration (`java { toolchain { ... } }`) tells Gradle to download or locate the correct JDK version, completely overriding `JAVA_HOME`. `sourceCompatibility` only controls language-level syntax, not the actual JDK binary used, so JAVA_HOME drift still silently causes problems.

questions.question: What does `jcmd <pid> VM.flags` output?

options.A: All Java source files loaded by the JVM
options.B: Active JVM flags — both those set via command line and defaults chosen by ergonomics
options.C: A thread dump of all running threads
options.D: The classpath used by the running process

questions.explanation: `jcmd VM.flags` is a Staff-level first step during on-call: it reveals the heap sizes, GC algorithm, and any non-default flags the JVM chose via ergonomics. Many JVM flags differ between JDK 11 and 17 defaults (G1GC became default in Java 9). Compare `VM.flags` on a broken pod vs a healthy one to find configuration drift.

questions.question: After a library upgrade, tests pass locally (Java 17) but fail in CI (Java 11) with `VerifyError: Bad type on operand stack`. What is the most likely root cause?

options.A: The library uses a Java 17 preview feature not available in Java 11
options.B: A bytecode-manipulation library (CGLIB, Lombok, ByteBuddy) generated class files that are valid per Java 17 rules but fail Java 11's stricter verifier
options.C: The `.class` files are corrupt because of a file-system encoding difference
options.D: Java 11 does not support generics

questions.explanation: `VerifyError` at runtime means the bytecode failed the JVM verifier — not a compile-time issue. Bytecode manipulation libraries sometimes generate code that passes newer verifier implementations but fails older ones. Fix: align Java version across all environments via Gradle toolchain or add the specific library version that fixed the verifier-compatible generation.

questions.question: A containerised Spring Boot app (Alpine-based, JDK 17) starts 40% slower in production than locally. The only flag set is `-Xmx512m`. What is the most likely JVM cause?

options.A: The JIT compiler is disabled in Docker
options.B: JVM ergonomics calculated a smaller heap based on the container CPU/memory limit, leaving too little room for JIT code cache, causing repeated deoptimisation
options.C: Alpine's musl libc causes `javac` to recompile all classes on startup
options.D: Docker networking latency delays class loading from the registry

questions.explanation: JVM ergonomics inspect available CPUs and memory. In a container with limits, the JVM may pick a small Code Cache (`-XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize`) causing the JIT to evict compiled code — the so-called "code cache full" deoptimisation loop. Add `-XX:+UseContainerSupport` (default on Java 10+) and set explicit `-XX:InitialCodeCacheSize` / `-XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize`. Check with `jcmd <pid> VM.flags | grep CodeCache`.

questions.question: You run `java -version` → 17.0.9, but `javac -version` → 11.0.21. Which consequence is most likely?

options.A: No consequence — the runtime version is what matters
options.B: Projects will compile against Java 11 API but run on Java 17, which may cause `NoSuchMethodError` if code depends on Java 17 APIs added after Java 11
options.C: The JVM will auto-select the matching `javac` version
options.D: Source files compile to Java 17 bytecode major version regardless of `javac` version

questions.explanation: A split `java`/`javac` version means PATH resolves them from different JDK installations. Code that uses Java 12–17 APIs (e.g., `String.isBlank()` added in Java 11, `Map.copyOf()`) will compile only if `javac` sees those APIs — with Java 11 `javac` you cannot even call Java 17-only methods. If somehow it compiles (via classpath tricks), you get `NoSuchMethodError` or `ClassNotFoundException` at runtime.

questions.question: What does `java -verbose:class MyApp 2>&1 | grep "java.lang.String"` help you diagnose?

options.A: Whether String pool is being used efficiently
options.B: Which ClassLoader and from which JAR/module `java.lang.String` was loaded
options.C: Whether String concatenation uses `StringBuilder` or `invokedynamic`
options.D: The number of String instances on the heap

questions.explanation: `-verbose:class` prints a line for every class load: `[Loaded java.lang.String from /path/to/jdk/jmods/java.base.jmod]`. If you see String loaded from an unexpected JAR in your classpath, you have a classpath pollution or shading conflict. A Staff engineer adds this flag to a failing startup to confirm the class loader source before filing a bug.

questions.question: What is JIT deoptimisation and when does it occur?

options.A: The JVM discards the JIT and falls back to interpretation when a profiling assumption is violated
options.B: The JIT downgrades from C2 to C1 when memory is low
options.C: Deoptimisation means the JVM disables GC to speed up a hot path
options.D: It occurs when `System.gc()` is called during JIT compilation

questions.explanation: C2 makes speculative optimisations based on runtime profiling (e.g., "this virtual call always goes to SubclassA"). When the assumption breaks (a second subclass is loaded), the JVM "deoptimises" — discards the native code, restores the interpreter state, and re-profiles. Frequent deoptimisations appear as `@deopt` in `-XX:+PrintCompilation` output and cause JIT warm-up latency spikes in production.

questions.question: Why does the JVM store class metadata in Metaspace (native memory) rather than the heap after Java 8?

options.A: Native memory is faster than heap memory
options.B: PermGen had a fixed upper bound causing `OutOfMemoryError`; Metaspace grows dynamically in native memory bounded only by the OS, eliminating a common tuning pain point
options.C: The GC cannot collect objects in native memory, which keeps metadata stable
options.D: Metaspace allows class metadata to be shared across JVM processes

questions.explanation: PermGen size was configured with `-XX:MaxPermSize` and commonly exhausted during hot-deploys of large applications (e.g., servlet containers redeploying WARs). Metaspace defaults to unlimited native memory growth, which shifts the failure mode from `PermGen OOM` to native memory exhaustion — easier to monitor with `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`. Note: classloader leaks still cause Metaspace to grow unboundedly.

questions.question: A multi-stage Dockerfile uses `openjdk:17-jdk` to build and `openjdk:17-jre` to run. A security scan flags the runtime image for JDK tools. What is the recommended Staff-level fix?

options.A: Use a single stage with the full JDK to avoid version mismatch
options.B: Switch the runtime stage to `eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine` or `gcr.io/distroless/java17` which contain only the JRE or a minimal runtime without shell or package manager
options.C: Add `-Dsecurity.scan=false` to the JVM flags
options.D: Copy only the `.class` files from the build stage to a scratch image

questions.explanation: Distroless and Alpine-JRE images drastically reduce attack surface: no shell, no package manager, no JDK tools like `jshell` or `jcmd`. A scratch image with raw `.class` files (option D) breaks because Spring Boot needs the full classpath and `java` launcher. Distroless images still include the JVM and standard library but nothing else — the right balance between security and functionality.

questions.question: Which `jcmd` subcommand shows heap memory breakdown (Eden, Survivor, Old Gen) without triggering a full GC?

options.A: `jcmd <pid> GC.run`
options.B: `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info`
options.C: `jcmd <pid> VM.native_memory`
options.D: `jcmd <pid> Thread.print`

questions.explanation: `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info` prints current heap region usage (Young/Old sizes, used vs capacity) without forcing a collection. `GC.run` triggers a full GC — dangerous in production if the heap is large (long pause). `VM.native_memory` tracks off-heap allocations. `Thread.print` produces a thread dump.

questions.question: On a new project, Gradle builds succeed locally but fail on CI with `error: package com.google.common.collect does not exist`. `JAVA_HOME` is correct. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: The JDK version differs between local and CI
options.B: The Guava dependency is in the `compileOnly` scope but needed at compile time — the CI cache is stale, causing the JAR to be absent
options.C: Guava is a JDK module that must be enabled with `--add-modules`
options.D: `JAVA_HOME` points to JRE; Guava requires the full JDK

questions.explanation: If a dependency is correctly declared but absent on CI, the Gradle dependency cache is typically the culprit — CI machines often use clean caches per build. Less commonly, the dependency is scope-mismatched (`runtimeOnly` instead of `implementation`). Guava is a third-party JAR, not a JDK module, so `--add-modules` and JDK version differences are red herrings.

questions.question: You are Staff engineer joining a new team. In week 1 you find `java -version` reports 11 on CI but `17` locally, and no Gradle toolchain is declared. What is your first move?

options.A: Upgrade CI agents to Java 17 immediately
options.B: Add a Gradle toolchain declaration pinning Java 17 with Foojay resolver, commit it, verify CI downloads and uses Java 17 — then decide whether to upgrade CI agent base images
options.C: Add `-source 11 -target 11` to `compileJava` options to match CI
options.D: Set `JAVA_HOME` in the CI environment variable to point to Java 17

questions.explanation: A Gradle toolchain declaration is the durable fix: it is version-controlled, reproducible, and removes dependence on machine-level `JAVA_HOME`. Hardcoding `-source 11` downgrades the compiler and masks the real problem. Setting `JAVA_HOME` in CI is fragile — it drifts as agents are re-imaged. Upgrading CI agents immediately without a toolchain declaration means the next agent image update reintroduces the mismatch.

sections.title: JDK, JRE, JVM — Interview Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| **JDK** | Ships `javac`, tools, and the runtime libraries | "The **JDK** is what compiles my sources and bundles the same bits I need to run locally." |
| **JRE** | Execution stack without `javac` | "A **JRE**-only box can run bytecode but cannot compile new sources." |
| **JVM** | OS process that loads bytecode | "Each `java` command spawns a **JVM** that verifies bytecode before `main`." |
| **Bytecode** | Portable IR stored in `.class` | "**Bytecode** lets one build artifact run anywhere a compatible **JVM** exists." |
| **`javac`** | Compile-time; emits class files | "`javac` is strictly compile-time; failures are syntax and type errors." |
| **`java` launcher** | Runtime entry | "The launcher configures **classpath**, GC, and native boot before `main`." |
| **`main` contract** | `public static void main(String[] args)` | "If `main` is wrong you get `NoSuchMethodError` after load, not compile errors." |
| **Classpath** | Ordered roots for `.class` / JAR | "**Classpath** issues produce `ClassNotFoundException` even when sources compile." |
| **Module path** | JPMS visibility | "**Module path** adds strong encapsulation beyond flat **classpath** scanning." |
| **JAVA_HOME** | JDK root for build tools | "Gradle reads **JAVA_HOME**; shells read **PATH** — both must agree." |
| **PATH hygiene** | JDK `bin` before stale shims | "I compare `where java` vs `where javac` when versions disagree on Windows." |
| **UnsupportedClassVersionError** | Major version mismatch | "That error means bytecode is newer than the runtime **JVM** major version." |
| **Single-file launch** | `java Hello.java` since 11 | "Single-file mode still ends in **bytecode**; I do not use it in CI." |
| **Bytecode verification** | Safety gate | "**Verification** rejects illegal stacks before any business logic executes." |
| **JIT** | HotSpot optimizes warm code | "**JIT** compiles hot methods to native code after interpreter warmup." |
